case_name: Seasonally variable surface water with pesticide degradates, diazinon, and lithium for groundwater recharge
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for surface water intended for groundwater recharge that balances seasonal pesticide variability, lithium control needs, and cost and energy constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A regional water agency plans to divert agriculturally influenced river water into recharge basins during wet-season high flows to augment a stressed aquifer. Monitoring shows recurring metolachlor degradates as the primary concern, episodic diazinon peaks after storm runoff, and persistent dissolved lithium that may limit recharge acceptance if not reduced. Multiple treatment routes are technically plausible, but the agency needs a solution that handles seasonal water quality swings without the high energy demand and brine management burden of full desalting systems.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Produce water suitable for managed aquifer recharge with strong reduction of pesticide risk and meaningful lithium control while maintaining stable operation over seasonal raw-water changes.
secondary_goals: limit energy consumption relative to full desalting treatment
maintain reasonable resilience to wet-season turbidity and DOC spikes
avoid overly complex operations for a medium-sized utility

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer solids residuals or replaceable media over large brine volumes; concentrate disposal options are limited and costly
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: treatment must tolerate seasonal turbidity, DOC, and pesticide pulse events without frequent process upset

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and lower particulate and NOM load before downstream polishing
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: provide stable turbidity removal and protect adsorption and membrane units during seasonal events
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: remove diazinon and reduce metolachlor degradates and DOC to decrease membrane fouling and pesticide risk
core_unit_functions.low_recovery_nanofiltration: provide partial dissolved-ion control including lithium reduction with lower energy than reverse osmosis
core_unit_functions.recharge_basin_post_conditioning: stabilize finished water quality for aquifer recharge compatibility

applicability_rationale: This hybrid route balances the key trade-off: adsorption and biologically active carbon are well suited for pesticide control, while a moderate-pressure membrane step adds the lithium reduction that adsorption alone cannot reliably provide.
Using solids removal and BAC upstream improves resilience to seasonal turbidity and DOC increases and reduces fouling pressure on the nanofiltration step.
Compared with full reverse osmosis, low-recovery nanofiltration better aligns with the stated energy sensitivity while still offering meaningful lithium reduction.
This option is more complex than adsorption-only treatment, but it provides a better overall fit when recharge acceptance depends on controlling both organics and a dissolved monovalent ion.
potential_risks: Lithium rejection with nanofiltration may be incomplete and must be validated against project-specific recharge criteria
Seasonal DOC spikes can still accelerate membrane fouling if pretreatment is not well optimized
A concentrate stream is still generated, though smaller and generally less burdensome than with high-recovery RO

core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish solids and stabilize filtered water quality
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: buffer episodic diazinon and storm-driven organic contaminant peaks
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: provide longer-term adsorption of diazinon and some metolachlor degradates while reducing DOC
core_unit_functions.recharge_basin_post_conditioning: deliver recharge-compatible water after organic contaminant polishing

applicability_rationale: This route is strong for seasonal pesticide control and is notably lower in energy demand than any membrane-centered alternative.
PAC plus GAC offers operational flexibility for runoff-driven diazinon pulses and can be effective for many trace organic compounds in surface water.

The main reason it does not rank first is that lithium is not meaningfully removed by activated carbon, so the option fits only if lithium reduction targets are moderate or handled through blending and recharge management outside the core train.
Where cost and energy are weighted more heavily than dissolved-ion removal, this could become the preferred route.
potential_risks: Metolachlor degradates are more polar and may exhibit lower adsorption affinity than parent pesticides, reducing certainty of deep removal
PAC feed control and GAC replacement planning are needed during high-load seasons

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove particulate matter and reduce oxidant and biofilter loading
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize diazinon and transform some organic micropollutants while improving biodegradability of NOM fractions
core_unit_functions.biological_filtration: remove biodegradable oxidation byproducts and polish residual organics
core_unit_functions.recharge_basin_post_conditioning: adjust final water for recharge application

applicability_rationale: Technically, this route addresses both major treatment needs: oxidation and biofiltration for pesticide management and a dedicated ion-exchange step for lithium.
It avoids large membrane concentrate volumes, which is attractive where brine disposal is difficult.
However, selective lithium removal from complex natural waters is a more specialized application than standard pesticide polishing and may bring media cost, regeneration, or replacement uncertainty.
The rank is lowered by process novelty and O&M sensitivity relative to the more established BAC plus nanofiltration pathway.
potential_risks: Selective lithium media performance may be affected by competing cations and site-specific water chemistry
Ozone system safety, off-gas handling, and dose control increase operational burden
Formation of oxidation byproducts requires careful monitoring and downstream biological polishing

core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide robust barrier pretreatment for RO feed protection
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: remove dissolved salts, lithium, and many trace organic contaminants at high efficiency
core_unit_functions.recharge_basin_post_conditioning: prepare treated water for aquifer recharge

applicability_rationale: This is the most technically comprehensive treatment route for simultaneous lithium and pesticide control.
It would likely provide the strongest finished-water quality margin for groundwater recharge where conservative removal of dissolved constituents is prioritized.
Despite that technical strength, it ranks lower because the scenario explicitly emphasizes energy sensitivity, cost control, and limited concentrate disposal options.
The option is included as a plausible but constraint-misaligned route.
potential_risks: High energy demand and membrane replacement costs may be disproportionate for this application
Brine management may be the controlling project constraint
Seasonal fouling pressure from surface water NOM requires strong pretreatment and attentive operation

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove particulate matter and reduce UV transmittance interference
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process_uv_h2o2: destroy susceptible organic micropollutants including diazinon
core_unit_functions.biological_filtration: remove biodegradable transformation products and polish treated water

applicability_rationale: AOP can be highly effective for diazinon and some trace organics, and biological filtration can help manage oxidation byproducts.
However, this route does not address lithium and may not be the most economical way to manage metolachlor degradates compared with adsorption-based systems.
For a project with high energy sensitivity, UV-based AOP is disadvantaged relative to carbon-based treatment unless destructive treatment is specifically required.
It is therefore technically capable for part of the problem but poorly aligned with the full multi-constraint objective.
potential_risks: UV dose requirements can rise when seasonal water quality lowers UV transmittance
Oxidation may transform rather than fully mineralize some compounds, requiring dependable downstream biological polishing

preferred_rank_pattern: A hybrid route that combines strong pesticide control with at least partial lithium removal should rank near the top
Adsorption-based treatment should appear as a strong lower-energy alternative but be penalized for poor lithium control
Full reverse osmosis should not rank first under high energy sensitivity and limited concentrate handling
At least one technically capable option should rank lower because it mismatches cost, energy, or residuals constraints

must_reflect: seasonal surface water variability requires robust pretreatment and tolerance to turbidity and DOC swings
energy sensitivity downgrades full desalting membrane solutions
cost pressure favors simpler carbon-based options unless lithium removal needs justify added complexity
concentrate and residuals considerations influence the ranking rather than raw removal capability alone

must_contain: why pesticide-focused carbon or biofiltration routes are attractive for surface water with seasonal runoff impacts
why lithium is difficult to remove with conventional adsorption and pushes consideration toward membranes or selective ion exchange
why nanofiltration may be favored over reverse osmosis when partial ion reduction is acceptable and energy is constrained
why metolachlor degradates may be more challenging than hydrophobic parent pesticides for adsorption-only treatment

should_mention: seasonal pesticide pulses after storm events
membrane fouling from DOC and turbidity
uncertainty in lithium removal performance for partial-rejection or selective media systems
